How we found themThe storiesThe five stepsThe full essay
extracted from the method essay, as a working manual

How to find a looted book

Five steps, drawn from fifteen years of doing it. The full account, with the false starts, is in the stories.

  1. 1
    Read the Dropsie card catalog, card by card

    In the Freidus Classification Shelf List, the card is often the only written record of a book’s source — including the note “Gift of Jewish Cultural Reconstruction.” Yet it records no more than about nine hundred JCR books, and nearly half of those are neither in the catalog nor on the shelf. For several dozen that remain, the card is the only JCR evidence there is — and some of those were printed after the war, trace to known individuals, or carry American ownership marks, each of which casts doubt on the note.

  2. 2
    Check the rare-manuscripts accession list

    A battered handwritten accession list is the sole evidence that Dropsie received six rare manuscripts. Nothing on the manuscripts themselves marks them as looted; without the list they would be invisible.

  3. 3
    Hunt the incunables named in the JCR report

    A JCR report stated that Dropsie received three incunables. With no titles given and no mark to match, every Dropsie incunable had to be pulled and inspected by hand to find the three.

  4. 4
    Reconnect empty bindings with their textblocks

    Rebinding discarded the old covers, and with them JCR bookplates and sometimes OAD or Nazi stamps. A marked empty binding can prove that a rebound, unmarked textblock belongs in the record — as with the Nedarim binding whose owner’s autograph matched a volume in the Rare Book Room that had been left out.

  5. 5
    Shelf-read the entire collection

    The most tedious step, and not a clean one. Books whose spine date is post-war but whose title page predates 1941 still carried OAD/JCR evidence, which forced the shelf read to widen and include spine dates through 1948.

Provenance research & scholarship by Bruce Nielsen, Ph.D. — Judaica Public Services Librarian & Archivist, Penn Libraries (Katz Center for Advanced Judaic Studies)A site built by Judaica DH at Penn Libraries
